rationale: The citing paper uses Sevilla and Townsend (2016) as key prior evidence that spatial or empty-space visual cues can elevate luxury-related evaluations and consumer response. It cites this work in the conceptual framing and again in the contribution section to position its main contribution as moving from static visual luxury cues to a dynamic cue (slow motion in video ads). Although it does not reuse the cited paper's specific mechanism or methods, the citation meaningfully supports the paper's theoretical positioning.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Sevilla and Townsend (2016) as part of the conceptual foundation showing that visual spacing cues can increase luxury perceptions and related outcomes. It builds from that static-cue literature to propose and test slow motion as a dynamic luxury cue in video advertising.
